name: Chicomecoatl
description: Chicomecoatl, also known as Chalchiuhcihuatl, is a prominent Aztec goddess of sustenance, agriculture, and fertility. Her name translates to "Seven Serpent," referencing the Aztec calendar and its cyclical nature, as well as the abundance she provides. She is a vital figure representing the life-giving forces of the earth, ensuring plentiful harvests and the continuation of life. Chicomecoatl is particularly associated with maize, the staple food of the Aztec people, and is often depicted holding ears of corn or surrounded by agricultural bounty. Offerings were made to her during various agricultural festivals to ensure a successful growing season and abundant yield. She is considered a protector of the home and family, bringing prosperity and well-being to those who honor her. Chicomecoatl is also linked to the female aspect of creation and the earth's generative power, embodying the nurturing and sustaining forces necessary for survival. Her importance underscores the Aztec society's deep connection to agriculture and their reliance on the earth's bounty. Some accounts portray her as a consort or aspect of Tlaloc, the rain god, further emphasizing her role in agricultural prosperity. She is venerated as a generous provider who ensures the survival and well-being of the Aztec people through her gifts of food and sustenance. Her depictions often include symbols of abundance, such as jade and precious stones, representing the richness and fertility of the earth.
subtitle: Goddess of Sustenance and Agriculture

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Maize
keyMyths.description: Chicomecoatl is believed to have provided the first maize to humankind. She taught the people how to cultivate it, ensuring their survival and prosperity.

keyMyths.title: Agricultural Festivals
keyMyths.description: During festivals dedicated to Chicomecoatl, offerings of food and precious objects were made to ensure a bountiful harvest. These rituals celebrated the goddess's life-giving power.

keyMyths.title: Association with Water
keyMyths.description: Chicomecoatl's connection to water emphasizes the importance of rain for agriculture. She is sometimes associated with Chalchiuhtlicue, the goddess of water.
keyMyths.source: Handbook to Life in the Aztec World
